headline: Binance is a centralized exchange: no public smart-contract repo, no audit links, and core custody logic is opaque and unverifiable on-chain

findings.text: Binance CEX operates as a centralized custodian. User balances are held in Binance-controlled wallets; there are no publicly identified smart-contract addresses representing the exchange's core trading, custody, or settlement logic that can be checked for bytecode verification. The protocol.github field is explicitly '(none recorded)' and protocol.audit_links is '(none recorded)', confirming no on-chain deployment surface has been published for review.

No GitHub repository is linked for Binance CEX. Binance does publish open-source tooling (e.g., binance-chain/bsc) for BNB Smart Chain infrastructure, but this does not represent the exchange's custody or trading engine. No source-to-repo correspondence can be established for the exchange itself.

No audit links are provided in protocol.audit_links. Binance has published Proof-of-Reserves attestations (e.g., via Mazars and then other firms after Mazars suspended crypto work in late 2022), but these are not smart-contract security audits and do not cover verifiability of deployed bytecode. No security audit of a specific smart-contract codebase at a specific commit was found.

No recognized Solidity audit firm has published an audit of Binance's core exchange contracts because no such contracts are publicly identified. Binance's BNB Chain (BSC) validators and bridge contracts have received separate audits, but those are distinct from the CEX itself.

Post-audit drift cannot be assessed because there are no audited contracts to compare against a current deployment. The exchange's matching engine and custody system are entirely off-chain/closed-source.

There is no proxy/implementation structure to assess because Binance CEX does not expose verifiable on-chain smart contracts for its core operations. Any on-chain addresses associated with Binance (e.g., hot/cold wallets visible on Etherscan) are plain EOA or multisig wallet addresses, not upgradeable contract systems with verifiable logic.

steelman.red: Binance CEX has no public source repo, no audit links, and no identified verifiable smart-contract deployment representing its core custody and trading logic — the exchange's operations are fundamentally opaque to independent on-chain verification.
steelman.orange: Binance publishes Proof-of-Reserves attestations and its BNB Chain infrastructure is open-source and audited, which provides partial transparency about reserve holdings even if the trading engine itself is not verifiable.
steelman.green: Binance's blockchain infrastructure (BSC, BNB Beacon Chain) is fully open-source and audited by recognized firms, and hot-wallet addresses are publicly visible on block explorers, giving some degree of on-chain transparency.

rationale.verdict: Choosing red because the verifiability checklist specifically requires deployed bytecode verified on a block explorer, a public source repo corresponding to the deployed contracts, and at least one audit from a recognized firm covering currently-deployed contracts. Binance CEX satisfies none of these: there is no identified smart-contract deployment for the exchange's core logic (V1), no linked GitHub repo (V2), and no audit links (V3/V4). The green and orange steel-mans apply to BNB Chain infrastructure, which is a separate protocol from the CEX itself and not the subject of this assessment.

evidence.shows: Binance's Proof-of-Reserves page — demonstrates that Binance's transparency efforts are attestation-based (reserve audits), not smart-contract bytecode verification. No contract addresses or audit PDFs of deployed exchange contracts are linked.
Etherscan's labeled Binance addresses — these are plain wallet/EOA addresses used for hot/cold storage, not verified smart contracts representing the exchange's trading or custody logic.
Binance announcement about Proof-of-Reserves methodology — confirms the approach is Merkle-tree-based reserve attestation, not smart-contract audit or on-chain verifiable logic.

Binance's public GitHub organization — contains API clients, SDK tooling, and chain infrastructure repos (e.g., for BSC), but no repository for the CEX's core trading engine, custody system, or settlement logic.

unknowns: V1: No canonical list of Binance CEX smart-contract addresses has been published; it is unknown whether any verifiable on-chain contracts exist for the exchange's core operations beyond plain wallet addresses.
V2: Cannot pin a commit SHA for Binance CEX trading/custody logic because no such public repository exists; the assessment is scope-limited by the absence of a repo, not by inability to diff.
V5: Post-audit drift is unassessable because no audited smart-contract codebase for the CEX has been identified.
V6: Proxy/implementation structure is unknown because no smart-contract deployment for the CEX's core logic has been publicly identified.

protocol_metadata.about: Binance is the world's largest centralized cryptocurrency exchange by trading volume, offering spot, margin, futures, and options markets across hundreds of trading pairs. Users deposit assets into Binance-controlled custodial wallets and trade via an off-chain order book and matching engine; the exchange also operates BNB Smart Chain (BSC), its own EVM-compatible blockchain. Binance publishes Merkle-tree-based Proof-of-Reserves attestations to provide partial transparency about the backing of user balances, but the core trading and custody infrastructure is closed-source.
